narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic records, test scores, and background details like yours. The model estimated a 73% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and resources. This decision hinged primarily on your early academic performance in school, particularly your low rankings in Year 1 and Year 3, where you placed in the bottom decile—far below the typical student's middle-range standing. Your Year 3 decile rank stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a persistent struggle with coursework that often correlates with bar exam challenges. Similarly, your Year 1 decile rank reinforced this concern, as it was also in the lowest group compared to peers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which aligns closely with the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing success. Other elements, like your lower-middle family income bracket and LSAT score of 31—below the average of 36.7—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Full-time enrollment plans, common among 93% of students, didn't sway things much either. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker early academic deciles that outweighed strengths like your solid undergrad grades. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law—many in similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements—but the model prioritizes patterns from past students. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger performance in advanced courses, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and perhaps seeking mentorship or prep programs tailored to bar success. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your prediction dramatically, opening doors at schools that value your unique path.
